articles.title: Equality Before Law
articles.description: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.

faqs.q: Does Article 14 mean everyone must be treated identically?
faqs.a: No. It permits reasonable classification based on intelligible differentia, as long as it has a rational link to the purpose of the law.

faqs.q: Can Article 14 be enforced against private individuals?
faqs.a: Generally it applies against the State, but courts have extended its principles to State instrumentalities and certain public functions.

legal_remedies: File a writ petition under Article 32 (Supreme Court) or Article 226 (High Court) challenging the arbitrary action
example_scenarios: A government scheme arbitrarily excludes a similarly situated group without reason
Unequal treatment by a government office for the same service
articles.citizen_explanation: This is your basic right to be treated equally by the government and the law — no one is above the law, and the law cannot single you out without a fair reason.

articles.title: Prohibition of Discrimination
articles.description: The State shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth. Special provisions for women, children, and socially/educationally backward classes (including SC/ST/OBC) are permitted.

faqs.q: Can reservations for women or backward classes violate Article 15?
faqs.a: No. Article 15(3) and 15(4) specifically allow the State to make special provisions for women, children, and backward classes.

faqs.q: Does this cover discrimination by private shops or hotels?
faqs.a: Article 15(2) specifically bars discrimination in access to shops, restaurants, wells, and public places, even if privately run.

legal_remedies: File a complaint with the police/local authority
Approach the High Court or Supreme Court under Articles 226/32
example_scenarios: Being denied entry to a restaurant or shop because of caste or religion
A college denying admission solely on grounds of sex
articles.citizen_explanation: The government and public places cannot turn you away or treat you worse just because of your religion, caste, race, sex, or where you were born.

articles.title: Equality of Opportunity in Public Employment
articles.description: All citizens have equal opportunity in matters of public employment. No discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, descent, place of birth, or residence. Reservation for backward classes in public posts is constitutionally permitted.

faqs.q: Does this apply to private company jobs?
faqs.a: No, Article 16 specifically covers public employment (government and State instrumentalities).

faqs.q: Is reservation in government jobs constitutional?
faqs.a: Yes, Article 16(4) permits reservation of posts for backward classes not adequately represented in services.

legal_remedies: File a complaint with the recruiting authority's grievance cell
Approach the High Court under Article 226 or the relevant Administrative Tribunal
example_scenarios: A government recruitment notice with an unjustified exclusion criterion
Denial of a government job due to caste-based bias outside reserved category rules
articles.citizen_explanation: If you're applying for a government job, you must be judged on merit and fair criteria — not turned away because of your religion, caste, sex, or birthplace.

articles.title: Abolition of Untouchability
articles.description: Untouchability is abolished and its practice in any form is forbidden. Enforcing any disability arising out of untouchability is an offence punishable by law.

faqs.q: Is practicing untouchability a criminal offence?
faqs.a: Yes. It is punishable under the Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 and the SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989.

faqs.q: What counts as enforcing untouchability?
faqs.a: Denying entry to temples, wells, shops, or public places, or social boycott, on the ground of caste.

legal_remedies: File a police complaint/FIR
File a complaint under the SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act if applicable
relevant_acts: Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989
example_scenarios: Being barred from a temple, well, or public space due to caste
Social boycott of a family on caste grounds
articles.citizen_explanation: Untouchability in any form is illegal — denying someone access to a place or treating them as inferior because of caste is a criminal offence.

articles.title: Abolition of Titles
articles.description: The State cannot confer any title (except military or academic distinctions) on any person. Citizens cannot accept titles from foreign states.

faqs.q: Are National Awards like Bharat Ratna titles?
faqs.a: Courts have held they are not titles in the constitutional sense; they are recognitions of merit and not hereditary titles.

legal_remedies: File a writ petition challenging conferment of a prohibited title
example_scenarios: A citizen using a royal/hereditary title in official contexts
Accepting a foreign award without government permission while holding public office
articles.citizen_explanation: India does not allow hereditary titles like 'Sir' or 'Lord' to be conferred by the State — everyone stands equal regardless of family lineage.

articles.title: Freedom of Speech and Other Freedoms
articles.description: Guarantees six freedoms to citizens: speech and expression, peaceful assembly, association, movement throughout India, residence and settlement, and practising any profession or trade. All are subject to reasonable restrictions in the interest of public order, morality, sovereignty, etc.

faqs.q: Is free speech absolute?
faqs.a: No. It is subject to reasonable restrictions under Article 19(2) — defamation, public order, decency, contempt of court, security of the State, etc.

faqs.q: Does this protect the right to protest?
faqs.a: Yes, peaceful assembly is protected, but the State can impose reasonable restrictions like requiring prior permission for public gatherings.

legal_remedies: File a writ petition under Article 32/226 against unreasonable restriction
Challenge an FIR/charge that criminalises protected speech
example_scenarios: Police denying permission for a peaceful protest without valid reason
Being booked for a social media post critical of the government
articles.citizen_explanation: You have the right to speak your mind, gather peacefully, move freely across India, live where you choose, and do any lawful business — though the government can impose fair, reasonable limits to protect public order.

articles.title: Protection in Respect of Conviction for Offences
articles.description: No person can be convicted except for violation of a law in force at the time of the act (no ex-post-facto law); no person can be prosecuted and punished twice for the same offence (no double jeopardy); no accused can be compelled to be a witness against himself (protection against self-incrimination).

faqs.q: Can police force you to confess?
faqs.a: No. Article 20(3) protects against being compelled to be a witness against yourself; forced confessions are inadmissible.

faqs.q: Can a new, harsher law apply to an act committed before it was passed?
faqs.a: No. You can only be punished under the law as it stood when the act was committed.

legal_remedies: Challenge the conviction/prosecution before the appellate court or High Court
File a writ petition if double jeopardy or coerced confession is alleged
relevant_acts: Code of Criminal Procedure / Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita
example_scenarios: Being tried again for an offence you were already acquitted or convicted of
Police pressuring a confession during interrogation
articles.citizen_explanation: You can't be punished under a law that didn't exist when you acted, tried twice for the same crime, or forced to testify against yourself.

articles.title: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty
articles.description: No person shall be deprived of life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law. Judicial interpretation has expanded this to include the right to live with dignity, livelihood, privacy, health, clean environment, education, and a speedy trial.

faqs.q: Does Article 21 cover the right to privacy?
faqs.a: Yes. The Supreme Court held in K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) that privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21.

faqs.q: Does it cover the right to livelihood?
faqs.a: Yes, courts have held that the right to life includes the right to livelihood — you cannot be arbitrarily deprived of your means of earning a living.

legal_remedies: File a writ petition (habeas corpus for illegal detention, or other writs) under Article 32/226
Approach NHRC for human rights violations by State agencies
example_scenarios: Illegal detention without following due procedure
Demolition of a livelihood/shelter without notice or due process
Unauthorised surveillance or data collection violating privacy
articles.citizen_explanation: This is the broadest and most powerful right you have — it protects your life, freedom, dignity, privacy, and ability to earn a living from arbitrary State action.

articles.title: Right to Education
articles.description: The State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children between the ages of 6 and 14 years, in such manner as the State may determine by law.

faqs.q: Which law implements this right?
faqs.a: The Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education (RTE) Act, 2009.

faqs.q: Can a school deny admission for not paying fees at the elementary level?
faqs.a: No, the RTE Act mandates free elementary education and a 25% reservation for economically weaker sections in private schools.

legal_remedies: File a complaint with the District Education Officer / School Management Committee
Approach the State Commission for Protection of Child Rights
relevant_acts: Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009
authorities: National/State Commission for Protection of Child Rights
example_scenarios: A government school refusing admission to a child aged 6-14
A private school denying the mandatory 25% EWS quota seats
articles.citizen_explanation: Every child between 6 and 14 has a constitutional right to free schooling — no child can be turned away or charged for this.
articles.source: Constitution of India, Part III (86th Amendment, 2002)

articles.title: Protection Against Arrest and Detention
articles.description: A person arrested must be informed of the grounds of arrest, has the right to consult and be defended by a lawyer of their choice, and must be produced before the nearest magistrate within 24 hours of arrest (excluding travel time), except in cases of preventive detention.

faqs.q: Can police hold someone for more than 24 hours without producing them in court?
faqs.a: No, except under specific preventive detention laws — ordinary arrests require production before a magistrate within 24 hours.

faqs.q: Can I refuse to answer police questions without a lawyer present?
faqs.a: You have the right to consult a lawyer of your choice before and during interrogation.

legal_remedies: File a habeas corpus petition for illegal detention
Lodge a complaint with the police complaints authority or NHRC
relevant_acts: Code of Criminal Procedure / Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita
example_scenarios: Being held by police for over 24 hours without being produced before a magistrate
Being denied access to a lawyer after arrest
articles.citizen_explanation: If you're arrested, the police must tell you why, let you have a lawyer, and bring you before a magistrate within 24 hours — they cannot hold you indefinitely without judicial oversight.

articles.title: Prohibition of Forced Labour and Trafficking
articles.description: Traffic in human beings, begar (forced labour without payment), and other similar forms of forced labour are prohibited and punishable by law. Compulsory service for public purposes (e.g. military service) is allowed without discrimination.

faqs.q: Is bonded labour illegal in India?
faqs.a: Yes, the Bonded Labour System (Abolition) Act, 1976 abolishes bonded labour and frees bonded labourers from their debt.

faqs.q: Does mandatory community service violate Article 23?
faqs.a: No, compulsory service for a public purpose like military conscription is specifically excluded, as long as it doesn't discriminate.

legal_remedies: File a complaint with the local police or District Magistrate
Approach the National/State Human Rights Commission
example_scenarios: Being forced to work without wages to repay a debt
Being trafficked or held against your will for labour
articles.citizen_explanation: No one can force you to work without pay or traffic you for labour — bonded and forced labour of any kind is illegal.

articles.title: Prohibition of Child Labour
articles.description: No child below the age of 14 years shall be employed to work in any factory, mine, or engaged in any other hazardous employment.

faqs.q: Can a 14-year-old work in any job?
faqs.a: Hazardous occupations remain barred even above 14 under the Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act; non-hazardous family enterprises are an exception.

faqs.q: What should I do if I see a child working in a factory?
faqs.a: Report it to the local Labour Department or Childline 1098.

legal_remedies: File a complaint with the Labour Department or Childline 1098
Report to local police for rescue and rehabilitation
authorities: National Commission for Protection of Child Rights
example_scenarios: A child below 14 working in a factory or mine
A minor employed in hazardous work like firecracker manufacturing
articles.citizen_explanation: Children under 14 cannot legally be made to work in factories, mines, or dangerous jobs — this protects their childhood and safety.

articles.title: Freedom of Religion
articles.description: All persons have freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practise, and propagate their religion, subject to public order, morality, health, and other fundamental rights.

faqs.q: Can the State regulate religious practices?
faqs.a: Yes, secular activities associated with religious practice can be regulated, and practices affecting public order, morality, or health can be restricted.

faqs.q: Does this include the right to convert others?
faqs.a: It includes the right to propagate one's religion, but several states have anti-conversion laws regulating conversions by force or inducement.

legal_remedies: File a writ petition if a religious practice is unreasonably restricted
Approach local police if your right to worship is obstructed
example_scenarios: Being prevented from practising your religion peacefully
Disruption of religious gatherings without lawful justification
articles.citizen_explanation: You are free to believe in, practise, and share your religion, as long as it doesn't disturb public order, morality, or health.

articles.title: Freedom to Manage Religious Affairs
articles.description: Every religious denomination has the right to establish and maintain institutions for religious and charitable purposes, manage its own affairs in matters of religion, and own and administer property, subject to public order, morality, and health.

faqs.q: Can the government take over management of a temple?
faqs.a: Courts allow State regulation of secular aspects (e.g. finances) of religious institutions, but not interference in core religious matters.

legal_remedies: File a writ petition challenging unlawful interference in religious management
example_scenarios: A religious denomination's internal management decision being overridden without legal basis
articles.citizen_explanation: Religious groups have the right to run their own institutions and manage their religious affairs without unnecessary government interference.

articles.title: Freedom from Religious Instruction in State Institutions
articles.description: No religious instruction shall be provided in any educational institution wholly maintained by State funds. In other institutions, no person can be compelled to receive religious instruction without their consent (or their guardian's, if a minor).

faqs.q: Can a private school run by a religious trust teach religion?
faqs.a: Yes, but if it receives State recognition/aid and is not wholly State-funded, students cannot be compelled to attend religious instruction without consent.

legal_remedies: File a complaint with the school/State Education Department
Approach the High Court if compelled without consent
example_scenarios: A government school conducting mandatory religious instruction
A student compelled to attend religious classes against their guardian's wishes
articles.citizen_explanation: Government-funded schools must stay neutral on religion, and no student can be forced into religious instruction without consent.

articles.title: Protection of Interests of Minorities
articles.description: Any section of citizens having a distinct language, script, or culture has the right to conserve it. No citizen shall be denied admission into any State-maintained or State-aided educational institution on grounds of religion, race, caste, or language.

faqs.q: Can a State-aided school deny admission based on a student's mother tongue?
faqs.a: No, Article 29(2) prohibits denial of admission on grounds of religion, race, caste, or language in State-maintained or aided institutions.

legal_remedies: File a complaint with the institution's grievance cell or Education Department
Approach the High Court under Article 226
example_scenarios: Denial of admission to a State-aided college based on a student's language or community
Suppression of a community's distinct script or language by State policy
articles.citizen_explanation: Your community's language, script, and culture are protected, and no government-aided institution can refuse you admission because of your religion, race, caste, or language.

articles.title: Right of Minorities to Establish Educational Institutions
articles.description: All minorities, whether based on religion or language, have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice.

faqs.q: Can the government interfere in the administration of a minority institution?
faqs.a: Reasonable regulation for maintaining educational standards is allowed, but the core right to administer cannot be taken away.

legal_remedies: File a writ petition if the right to establish/administer is unreasonably restricted
example_scenarios: A State authority unreasonably blocking a minority community from establishing a school
Excessive interference in the internal administration of a minority-run institution
articles.citizen_explanation: Religious and linguistic minorities have a constitutional right to set up and run their own schools and colleges.

articles.title: Right to Constitutional Remedies
articles.description: Guarantees the right to move the Supreme Court directly for enforcement of Fundamental Rights, through writs: habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, certiorari, and quo warranto. Often called the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution.
synonyms: right to approach the supreme court

faqs.q: What is the difference between Article 32 and Article 226?
faqs.a: Article 32 lets you approach the Supreme Court directly; Article 226 gives the same writ power to High Courts, and is often faster/more accessible.

faqs.q: Can Article 32 be suspended?
faqs.a: Yes, during a National Emergency under Article 359, the enforcement of Article 32 (along with other rights) can be suspended, except Articles 20 and 21.

legal_remedies: File a writ petition directly in the Supreme Court (Article 32) or High Court (Article 226)
example_scenarios: Any Fundamental Right (Articles 14-30) being violated by State action, with no other adequate remedy
Illegal detention requiring an urgent habeas corpus writ
articles.citizen_explanation: If any of your fundamental rights are violated, this is your direct ticket to the Supreme Court (or High Court) to get it enforced — it's the right that makes all other rights enforceable.
